title: Process of Various Methods Review
narrationScript: Every penetrant job runs the same skeleton: preclean, penetrant and dwell, removal of excess, developer, development dwell, inspection, postclean. The four standard methods differ at exactly one step — removal — and that is where sensitivity is won or lost. Method A penetrants carry a built-in emulsifier, so a direct water spray takes them off. But the penetrant inside the flaw is just as washable, which makes overwash the standing risk: wash to background removal, then stop. Methods B and D add a separate chemistry. Lipophilic emulsifier works by diffusion into the penetrant layer, so contact time sets the depth — dip it, never agitate it, rinse on schedule. Hydrophilic remover acts like a detergent at the surface, after a pre-rinse, governed by concentration and contact time. That metered removal is why the highest-sensitivity systems are postemulsifiable. Method C is wipe discipline: dry cloth, then a solvent-dampened cloth — never flood the part. And classify every system the way the standards do: type, method, sensitivity level — plus the dual-sensitivity family that reads in visible and fluorescent modes.

sections.heading: One Skeleton, Four Removal Strategies
sections.body:
At Level I you memorized the seven-step skeleton; at Level II you own it. Every penetrant examination — aerosol kit on a scaffold or automated line in an engine shop — runs the same sequence: **preclean → penetrant application + dwell → removal of excess → developer → development dwell → inspection → postclean**.

The standard methods are named for the single step where they truly differ: how excess penetrant comes off the surface. Classification practices such as ASTM E1417 assign the letters:

- **Method A — water-washable**: the penetrant carries a built-in emulsifier, so water removes it directly
- **Method B — postemulsifiable, lipophilic**: a separate oil-base emulsifier is applied as a timed step
- **Method C — solvent-removable**: excess penetrant is wiped away by hand, finished with a solvent-dampened cloth
- **Method D — postemulsifiable, hydrophilic**: a water-base remover acts after a plain-water pre-rinse

Application, development, and inspection are essentially common property across all four. Removal is the contested ground — the same step must take *everything* off the surface while taking *nothing* out of the flaw. Trace each method with that tension in mind and the critical control points locate themselves.

sections.caption: Four methods, one skeleton — every lane shares application and development; the solid-blue removal step is where each method's sensitivity is won or lost.

sections.heading: Method A — Water-Washable: Fast, Direct, Unforgiving
sections.body:
The sequence is the shortest of the four: **penetrant + dwell → water wash → dry → developer** (aqueous developer, when used, goes on wet before the dryer). One step does all the removal, and nothing protects the flaw-trapped penetrant during it except your discipline.

The wash is controlled, not casual. Typical procedure limits run a **coarse water spray at or below 40 psi** with water between about **50 and 100 °F**, for the minimum time that clears the background. On a fluorescent line the wash station has UV-A lighting so the operator watches the background dissolve and **stops at background removal** — not one pass later. Because the emulsifier is blended into the penetrant itself, the penetrant sitting *inside* a flaw is exactly as washable as the excess on the surface: spray past the stopping point and shallow, wide-open flaws give up their penetrant first. That is **overwash**, it is invisible after the fact, and the only recovery is full cleaning and reprocessing. Underwash is the opposite failure — residual background that glows or stains across the whole surface and buries real indications.

Why choose Method A at all? Speed, cost, and geometry. Baskets of small parts, rough castings, threads, keyways, and deep recesses wash clean in one operation that would take hours of wiping.

sections.heading: Methods B and D — Postemulsifiable: Removal You Can Meter
sections.body:
Postemulsifiable penetrants are formulated **without** emulsifier — by themselves they shrug off water. The penetrant in the flaw therefore stays put through removal, protected by its own chemistry, while you convert only the **surface layer** into something washable. Two chemistries do that conversion:

- **Method B — lipophilic (oil-base) emulsifier.** Applied by **dipping or flowing only — never spraying or brushing** — it works by *diffusing into* the penetrant layer. Depth of emulsification is set by contact time, typically tens of seconds up to a common limit of about **3 minutes**, and the part is **not agitated**: stirring drives emulsifier unevenly toward the flaw-trapped penetrant. Drain orientation matters, because emulsifier keeps acting wherever it pools. The water rinse is what stops the clock.
- **Method D — hydrophilic (water-base) remover.** A **plain-water pre-rinse** first strips the bulk of the surface penetrant, protecting the remover bath and leaving a thin, even film. The remover — a detergent-like solution at a qualified concentration, commonly up to about **35 % for immersion** and far leaner for spray, around **5 %** — then acts **at the surface only**, with mild agitation often permitted, for a contact time commonly held to **2 minutes or less**, ended by a final rinse. Concentration is a documented process control, checked with a refractometer, because the bath drifts as it loads with penetrant.

Metered removal is the payoff: postemulsifiable processing carries the highest-sensitivity fluorescent systems on critical hardware, and Method D's surface-only action and tighter controls have made it the standard on modern aerospace lines.

sections.caption: Lipophilic emulsifier dissolves down into the penetrant layer — time sets depth. Hydrophilic remover scrubs at the surface — concentration and time set strength. Neither may reach the penetrant in the flaw.

sections.heading: Method C — Solvent-Removable: The Discipline of the Wipe
sections.body:
Method C trades the processing line for a cloth, which makes it the method of field welds, touch-up checks, and parts too large to dunk. The sequence: **penetrant + dwell → dry wipe → solvent-dampened wipe → nonaqueous developer** on a dry surface.

The discipline is all in the wiping. First take off as much as possible with **clean, dry, lint-free cloths**. Then finish with a cloth **dampened — not dripping — with remover**, turning to a clean face as it loads. The solvent goes on the cloth, **never onto the part**: flooded or sprayed-on solvent runs into flaw openings and dissolves the trapped penetrant out, killing indications more efficiently than any overwash. Qualified removers come in classes — **Class 1 halogenated, Class 2 nonhalogenated, Class 3 special application** — selected for material compatibility as much as for performance.

Method C pairs naturally with Type II visible penetrant for weld inspection under ordinary light, and with Type I for local fluorescent checks. Its developer is almost always **nonaqueous (forms d and e)** — the most sensitive common developer form — so a careful inspector achieves excellent sensitivity on a small area. The cost is labor: wiping down a turbine disk is not a plan, and consistency depends on hands, not plumbing.

sections.caption: Removal control points by method — typical limits shown; the written procedure governs.
rows: Emulsifier built into the penetrant; direct water spray
Spray commonly ≤ 40 psi, water about 50–100 °F; wash watched under proper lighting; stop at background removal
Overwash — the same spray strips flaw-trapped penetrant
Oil-base emulsifier diffuses into the surface penetrant layer
Dip or flow on only; contact timed, commonly ≤ 3 min; no agitation; drain orientation; rinse ends the clock
Over-emulsification from excess time, pooling, or stirring
Hand wiping; solvent only dampens the final cloth
Dry wipe first; cloth dampened, never saturated; no flooding or spraying solvent onto the part
Solvent flushing dissolves penetrant out of the flaw
Detergent-like water-base remover acts at the surface
Pre-rinse first; concentration qualified (immersion commonly ≤ 35 %, spray around 5 %) and verified; contact commonly ≤ 2 min; final rinse
Concentration drift or long contact scrubs shallow flaws empty

sections.title: Removal decides sensitivity
sections.body: All four methods apply penetrant the same way and develop it the same way — what separates them is how much protection flaw-trapped penetrant has while the excess comes off. Method A gives it none: the penetrant is washable everywhere, including inside the flaw. Method C protects it only as well as the inspector's wipe discipline. Methods B and D meter the removal chemistry by time and concentration so that only the surface layer ever becomes removable — which is why the highest-sensitivity systems ride on postemulsifiable processing. Know where the protection comes from in the method you are running, and you know which control to guard.

sections.heading: Classifying the System — Type, Method, Level, and Dual Sensitivity
sections.body:
A complete penetrant system reads like a part number, and a Level II should parse it on sight. A typical procedure call-out: **Type I, Method D, Sensitivity Level 3, Form a developer, Class 2 remover**.

- **Type** — I fluorescent, II visible, per classification practices such as ASTM E1417 and the materials specification AMS 2644
- **Method** — A water-washable, B postemulsifiable lipophilic, C solvent-removable, D postemulsifiable hydrophilic
- **Sensitivity level** — ½ (lowest) through 4 (ultrahigh), assigned to **Type I** systems by comparative testing under AMS 2644; Type II systems are not graded into sensitivity levels
- **Developer form** — a dry powder, b water-soluble, c water-suspendible, d and e nonaqueous, f special
- **Remover class** — 1 halogenated, 2 nonhalogenated, 3 special application

The topical outline also classifies systems by removal family — solvent-removable, water-washable, postemulsifiable (water-base hydrophilic or oil-base lipophilic) — plus one more: **dual sensitivity**. Dual-sensitivity (dual-mode) penetrants carry a dye that shows visible color under white light **and** fluoresces under UV-A. The same processed part can be screened visibly, then examined fluorescent — the fluorescent reading is the more sensitive of the two modes. They are a specialized family: ASTM E1417 and AMS 2644 qualify materials as Type I **or** Type II only, while international practice such as ISO 3452 lists a dual-purpose type — so expect dual-mode systems only where a specification explicitly invokes them, with the governing mode stated in the written procedure.

figures.title: Process flow by method: where removal control lives
Lipophilic diffusion vs hydrophilic detergent action

interactive.title: Classify the Removal Family
interactive.intro: Each statement describes exactly one removal family. File it where it belongs — and know why.

items.label: The penetrant carries a built-in emulsifier
items.why: Method A penetrants are formulated self-emulsifying — that is what lets plain water remove them directly, and what makes overwash the standing risk.

items.label: A separate, timed step makes the surface layer washable
items.why: Postemulsifiable penetrant is not water-washable until emulsifier or remover acts on it; that timed conversion step is the sensitivity control.

items.label: Dry wipe first, then a solvent-dampened cloth
items.why: Method C removal is mechanical: most penetrant comes off with dry, lint-free cloths, and a dampened — never saturated — cloth clears the rest.

items.label: A plain-water pre-rinse comes before the remover bath
items.why: Method D includes a pre-rinse to strip bulk penetrant so the hydrophilic remover works on a thin, even film and the bath stays serviceable.

items.label: No agitation — the chemistry works by diffusion, and time sets the depth
items.why: That is Method B's lipophilic emulsifier: it dissolves into the penetrant layer over time, and stirring drives it unevenly toward flaw-trapped penetrant.

items.label: Straight from penetrant dwell to the water spray — nothing added in between
items.why: Only Method A washes directly: no emulsification step and no pre-rinse, because the remover is already blended into the penetrant.

items.label: First choice for a one-off weld check in the field with no water or wash station
items.why: Method C runs from aerosol cans and cloths with no plumbing, which is why it owns field, touch-up, and large-part local inspections.

items.label: Remover concentration verified — typically by refractometer — as a documented process control
items.why: Hydrophilic remover (Method D) is a water solution whose strength drifts as it loads with penetrant; concentration checks against the qualified limit are routine.

checkQuestions.question: A fluorescent procedure specifies Method D. Which sequence is correct after the penetrant dwell?
options: Hydrophilic remover dip with agitation, then dry, then a water wash
Solvent-dampened wipe, then nonaqueous developer
Plain-water pre-rinse, hydrophilic remover at the qualified concentration for a timed contact, final rinse, then dry
Direct water wash to background removal, then a remover dip to polish the background
checkQuestions.explanation: Method D inserts two steps between dwell and drying: a pre-rinse that strips bulk penetrant and protects the remover bath, then a timed, concentration-controlled remover contact stopped by a final rinse. A solvent wipe is Method C, and washing to background removal before the remover defeats the method's whole purpose.

checkQuestions.question: Why is a lipophilic emulsifier applied as a timed dip and never agitated?
options: It acts by diffusing into the penetrant layer, so contact time sets emulsification depth — and agitation drives it unevenly toward the flaw-trapped penetrant
Agitation makes the emulsifier foam, which blocks UV-A light during the wash
Agitation cools the emulsifier below its qualified temperature range
The emulsifier is water-based and separates out of solution when stirred
checkQuestions.explanation: Lipophilic (oil-base) emulsifier works by diffusion into the penetrant layer. Time in contact is the only intended control of how deep that action reaches, so the dip is clocked — commonly to about 3 minutes or less — and the part is not stirred, because agitation accelerates and unevens the action toward what is inside the flaws.

checkQuestions.question: On a Method A fluorescent line, the wash operator keeps spraying until every trace of background is gone, then adds a few extra passes 'to be sure.' What should a Level II expect?
options: Improved sensitivity, because a perfectly clean background means cleaner indications
Overwash — the emulsifier is built into the penetrant, so continued spraying strips penetrant from the flaws themselves
No effect, because penetrant inside a flaw is not water-washable in Method A
Underwash, because extra spraying redeposits emulsified penetrant on the surface
checkQuestions.explanation: A water-washable penetrant is exactly as removable inside the flaw as on the surface — that is the method's defining weakness. The wash is watched under UV-A and stopped at background removal; the extra passes strip shallow flaws first, the damage is invisible afterward, and the only recovery is full reprocessing.

checkQuestions.question: Which removal technique is correct for Method C on a structural weld in the field?
options: Flood the weld with solvent remover, then towel it dry
Spray remover directly onto the surface and immediately apply developer
Rinse with low-pressure water, then mist the surface with solvent
Wipe with clean, dry, lint-free cloths first, then finish with a solvent-dampened cloth — never apply solvent directly to the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Method C removal is dry wiping followed by a dampened-cloth wipe. Solvent applied directly to the surface — by flooding, spraying, or misting — runs into flaw openings and dissolves out the trapped penetrant, destroying indications. Water washing is not part of the solvent-removable process.

checkQuestions.question: What distinguishes a dual-sensitivity penetrant system?
options: It is qualified at two AMS 2644 sensitivity levels, depending on which developer form is applied
Its dye shows visible color under white light and also fluoresces under UV-A, so one processed part can be read in either mode
It contains two penetrants applied in sequence, lower sensitivity first
It is a Type II penetrant qualified at both Sensitivity Level 3 and Level 4
checkQuestions.explanation: Dual-sensitivity (dual-mode) penetrants combine visible color contrast with fluorescent response — the fluorescent reading is the more sensitive of the two modes. A qualified material's sensitivity level is fixed by AMS 2644 testing, not by developer choice, and Type II systems carry no sensitivity-level grading at all.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §1.2, §1.4, §2.3 (process of various methods; penetrant system classification)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (types, methods, and process requirements, general reference)
SAE AMS 2644, Inspection Material, Penetrant (material classification and sensitivity levels, general reference)
ASTM E165/E165M, liquid penetrant examination practice (general reference)
